ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian fried beefball (bakso penyet). Closest defensible parent is 'meatball food product' (FOODON_03315595). Deep-fried preparation and sambal accompaniment are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (meat-based composite dish) used as closest defensible parent anchor. Specific bakso penyet entry does not exist in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Indonesian fried beefball dish. SNOMED CT coverage of Southeast Asian composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of bakso penyet typically weighs approximately 400 g and includes 5–7 deep-fried beef meatballs, fried batter pieces, tempeh, tofu, spicy sambal, and a small side salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Meatballs, beef, cooked (SR Legacy) and composite dish estimation using HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for bakso penyet exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef meatball entries (FDC ID 174032), deep-fried tempeh and tofu data (HPB Singapore), and sambal composition data (HPB Singapore). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved sources and found to be broadly consistent. Energy 240 kcal/100g, protein 14g, fat 17.5g, carbohydrate 6g are plausible for a deep-fried beef meatball composite dish. Values retained with medium confidence. Trans fat 0.25g/100g is plausible for deep-fried preparation using partially hydrogenated oils common in commercial frying. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available in approved sources for this composite dish and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed meat-and-starch composite dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bakso penyet. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous composite meat-and-starch deep-fried dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains tapioca flour and wheat flour batter (moderate GI starch components) combined with beef protein and fat which attenuate glycaemic response. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls in the low category (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Carbohydrate per 100g is 6g; per 400g serving carbohydrate is 24g. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 24) / 100 = 13.2 (medium GL). Estimation is conservative and consistent with published GI values for similar fried meat-starch composite dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 475 mg/100g (1900 mg per 400g serving) — driven by shrimp paste (terasi), added salt, and soy-based condiments; saturated fat 5g/100g compounds cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit bakso penyet due to sodium content of approximately 1900 mg per standard serving (exceeding the 1500–2000 mg daily target in one meal); request reduced shrimp paste and salt in sambal preparation, and consider grilled rather than deep-fried beefballs to reduce saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-frying adds significant fat (17.5g/100g) and refined carbohydrate from wheat and tapioca flour batter with low dietary fibre (1g/100g), associated with impaired postprandial glycaemic control per ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit bakso penyet due to the high fat content from deep-frying and refined starch batter contributing to elevated postprandial glucose and insulin resistance; request baked or grilled beefballs without batter, retain tempeh (beneficial fermented soy), and pair with non-starchy vegetables to improve the glycaemic profile of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5g/100g (20g per 400g serving), trans fat 0.25g/100g, cholesterol 50mg/100g, and sodium 475mg/100g from deep-frying and shrimp paste collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of bakso penyet due to the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium per serving, which collectively raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; if consumed, request grilled preparation without batter, reduce sambal quantity, and increase the proportion of tempeh and salad components.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; beef contains arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; sodium 475mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried bakso penyet as AGEs from frying and arachidonic acid from red beef meatballs amplify joint inflammation; request grilled or baked preparation, consider substituting beef with chicken or fish, and retain tempeh for its anti-inflammatory fermented soy properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso Penyet (Fried Beefball) is an Indonesian-origin dish popular in Singapore, consisting of handmade beef meatballs (bakso) that are deep-fried until crispy, then served smashed or whole alongside deep-fried wheat-and-tapioca flour batter pieces, deep-fried tempeh, deep-fried tofu, spicy sambal made from chilli, shrimp paste (terasi), garlic, and lime, and a fresh side salad of cucumber, tomato, and cabbage. The dish is characterised by a crispy exterior on the beefballs contrasting with a dense, springy interior typical of tapioca-bound beef meatballs. The sambal provides intense heat and umami depth from fermented shrimp paste. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 240 kcal per 100g, with high fat (17.5g/100g) from deep-frying, moderate protein (14g/100g) from beef and soy components, and low carbohydrate (6g/100g) from batter. Sodium is elevated at 475mg/100g, primarily from shrimp paste and added salt. Saturated fat is 5g/100g and trans fat is 0.25g/100g from commercial frying oil. Dietary fibre is low at 1g/100g. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. Clinically, it is unsuitable for hypertension (high sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs from deep-frying, arachidonic acid from beef). For type 2 diabetes, the high fat and refined starch batter are concerns. Tempeh is a beneficial fermented soy component with anti-inflammatory and prebiotic properties. A standard serving is one plate (approximately 400g), providing approximately 960 kcal, 56g protein, 70g fat, and 24g carbohydrate.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for bakso penyet exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are broadly consistent with approved-source analogues. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium.

Calcium 15mg/100g, iron 1.5mg/100g, cholesterol 50mg/100g, vitamin_c 5mg/100g retained from input record and consistent with approved-source analogues for this dish type. Two portions listed in input (1 plate = 400g; 7 pieces with sambal = 200g); default serving used is 400g (1 plate) for GL calculation. Input had two entries with is_default: true — corrected to use 400g as primary default. Indonesian cuisine: Indonesian local name mandatory and present (Bakso Penyet). This dish is served in Singapore and the record is Singapore-market facing; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names have been added to support Singapore multilingual retrieval. Chinese names provided as supplementary for Singapore market context. GI value 55 falls at the boundary of the low category per University of Sydney GI scale (low: ≤55); gi_category set to 'low' consistent with this classification. Ontology match_type values set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] — no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for bakso penyet.
